corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The Jean Genet papers document the creative process, literary career, and involvement in political and social causes of French author and political activist Jean Genet. The collection contains manuscript material of published and unpublished literary works—including screenplays and essays—alongside political writings, correspondence, personal papers, and other documents related to Genet. The collection spans the years 1940-1988, with the majority of materials dating from 1944 to 1983.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Acquired by purchase from various sources. Source information is indicated below and recorded at the subseries and file levels.

Beinecke Book Fund, 2019. Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2021. Purchased from Amie Stoppard on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2023. Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2023. Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, and on the Frederick W. Hilles Fund, 2024.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is organized into three series: I. Works, 1944-1984. II. Correspondence and Collected Materials, 1940-1988. III. Personal Papers, 1951-1966.

The collection combines several accessions of materials by or related to Jean Genet, acquired by the Beinecke Library between 1974 and 2024. Contextual information regarding the origins, ownership, and custody of items or groupings of materials is included in the description of the material.

bioghist.head: Jean Genet (1910-1986)
bioghist.p: Jean Genet (1910-1986) was a French novelist, poet, playwright, essayist, and political activist.

Born in Paris, France, Genet was the son of Camille Gabrielle Genet, a 22-year-old single maidservant. Struggling to support herself, she placed her son in the care of social services when he was just seven months old. In July 1911, Genet became a ward of the state and was sent to live with a foster family in Alligny-en-Morvan. Although he excelled in school, he also engaged in petty theft during his youth. In September 1926, after multiple criminal offenses, Genet was sent to the Penal Colony of Mettray. In March 1929, he enlisted in the French Army in order to gain an early release from Mettray. Genet served in Lebanon, Syria, Morocco, and Algeria, but deserted his post in June 1936. He then wandered around Europe as a vagabond, petty thief, and prostitute—experiences later recounted in his book Le Journal du voleur ( The Thief's Journal ). Genet returned to France in July 1937, continuing to live off thefts (including stealing and reselling rare books), and spent several years in and out of prison. In 1938, he was dishonorably discharged from the Army for indecency.

Genet began writing during his periods of incarceration. In 1942, he completed the poem Un Condamné à Mort ( The Man Sentenced to Death ) and his first novel, Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs ( Our Lady of the Flowers ). His work began to gain recognition within literary circles. Paul Morihien, secretary to Jean Cocteau, and Robert Denoël, an associate of Morihien, signed a contract with Genet in March 1943, and published Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs in a limited, clandestine edition later that year. Around this time, Genet also contacted Marc Barbezat, owner of the publishing house L'Arbalète, who would become one of his primary publishers.

Between 1944 and 1949, Genet wrote four novels— Miracle de la rose ( Miracle of the Rose ), Pompes funèbres ( Funeral Rites ), Querelle de Brest , and Journal d'un voleur ( A Thief's Journal ). In 1948, Genet was convicted of theft. Having already been convicted ten times, he faced an automatic sentence of life imprisonment. Prominent figures, including Jean Cocteau and Jean-Paul Sartre, appealed on his behalf, ultimately securing a presidential pardon.

After turning to theater, Genet wrote Haute Surveillance ( Deathwatch ) and Les Bonnes ( The Maids ) in 1947, followed by Splendid's in 1948. Throughout the 1950s, he focused on writing plays that were experimental and increasingly political: Le Balcon ( The Balcony ), Les Nègres ( The Blacks ), and Elle ( Her ) in 1955, and Les Paravents ( The Screens ) between 1956 and 1961, a play about the Algerian War of Independence. Directed by Roger Blin, Les Paravents premiered at the Odéon Theatre in 1966.

Genet also wrote several screenplays. In 1950, he wrote and directed the short film Un Chant d'Amour , depicting the fantasies of a gay male prisoner and his prison warden. In 1951, he wrote a screenplay in the form of a story with dialogues, Les Rêves interdits ou l'autre versant du rêve ( The Forbidden Dreams or the Other Side of the Dream ). The story was adapted into the 1966 film Mademoiselle by Tony Richardson. Genet also wrote the screenplay for Le Bagne ( The Penal Colony ) (1952–1954); Divine , an adaptation of his first novel Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs (for David Bowie in 1975); La Nuit venue/Le Bleu de L'oeil ( The Night Has Come/The Blue of the Eye ) (1976–1978); and Le Langage de la muraille: cent ans jour après jour ( The Language of the Walls: One Hundred Years Day After Day ), a TV series for Antenne 2 that was abandoned in 1982 before production.

After the completion of Les Paravents , Genet retreated from the literary scene. He became severely depressed following the death of his companion Abdallah Bentaga in 1964, and attempted suicide in Italy during the spring of 1967. He re-entered public life at a time of social unrest and wrote an homage to Daniel Cohn-Bendit after the events of May 1968. He also participated in demonstrations highlighting the living conditions of immigrants in France.

Genet felt a strong sense of solidarity with the oppressed and marginalized, both in France and abroad, and his political engagement took many forms over his lifetime. In the 1970s, he worked with Michel Foucault's Groupe d'information sur les prisons (GIP), visited members of the Black Panther Party in the United States, supported Angela Davis and George Jackson, and later spent time in Palestinian refugee camps. Genet also wrote articles and essays and gave speeches in support of revolutionary movements. In 1983, Genet began Un Captif amoureux ( Prisoner of Love ), which recounts his experiences with Palestinian fighters and Black Panther Party members. He corrected the proofs just before his death in Paris in April 1986.

bioghist.head: Jean Genet Writing Process
bioghist.p: Jean Genet's working method was distinctive. He typically wrote in school notebooks and would hand his drafts over to typists. After receiving the typed text, he would revise it, often by adding new passages. He did this either by annotating the typed pages, inserting handwritten additions on loose sheets, or pasting autograph corrections onto the typed document, essentially creating a new manuscript. The revised version would then be typed again, and the resulting typescript would be corrected, enriched, and restructured. This method highlights the importance of both handwritten and typed manuscripts in Genet's writing process. Genet's method also involved mixing earlier drafts, whether handwritten or typed, making it difficult to trace the precise evolution of his works.

Several groups of materials in the collection offer an in-depth look at this process. For example, the materials related to Le Bagne in the Jean Genet papers provide valuable insight into both his writing process and the history of one of his works. Genet wrote the screenplay for Le Bagne between 1952 and 1954, but he set it aside in 1956. He later began writing a theatrical version in 1958, only to destroy the final version after the suicide of his partner, Abdallah Bentaga, in 1964. However, two sets of drafts of Le Bagne survived: one he had given to his publisher, Marc Barbezat, in 1963, and another to his literary agent and American translator, Bernard Frechtman. In 1990, Barbezat acquired Frechtman's copy at auction, using both drafts to create a posthumous theatrical version of Le Bagne , which was published in 1994 by L'Arbalète.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: The following note provides an overview of the custodial history of materials by or related to Jean Genet. Drafts of Genet's works were often kept in the papers of his publishers and editors (including Paul Morihien, Marc Barbezat, and Monique Lange), literary agent and translator Bernard Frechtman, as well as patrons and friends, such as collector Jacques Guérin and attorney Roland Dumas. Over time, much of this literary material was dispersed through public auctions or private sales.

Significant material related to Genet has been acquired over the decades by art collectors and bibliophiles Pierre Bergé (1930-2017) and Geneviève and Jean Paul Kahn (1931-2018). These two important collections were gradually dispersed through multiple auctions between 2015 and October 2024 for Bergé—whose proceeds went to charity and the Pierre Bergé-Yves Saint Laurent Foundation—and from November 2019 to June 2024 for the Kahns. Materials from both collections are in the holdings of the Beinecke.

Another key custodian of Genet's legacy was attorney Roland Dumas (1922-2024). In 1986, just two weeks before his death, Genet entrusted two suitcases filled with a mix of personal artifacts, manuscripts, and notes, to his close friend. These materials are mostly dated from the period 1967-1982, and include works that were thought to have been destroyed or had been unknown, such as drafts of the filmscripts for La Nuit and Divine . Dumas donated the suitcases to L'Institut mémoires de l'édition contemporaine (IMEC) in November 2019, where they joined the Fonds Jean Genet at the institute.

The Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library holds a substantial collection of Genet's papers. The materials come from multiple creators, owners, and collectors. To facilitate the tracing of provenance, information regarding the custodial history of specific groups of materials or individual manuscripts is included in the collection description.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Jean Genet Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The collection was reprocessed in January 2025 by Sandrine Guérin to consolidate previously cataloged and newly acquired materials and to represent the collection through a single online guide.

Former titles and call numbers: Jean Genet writings, 1946-1966. (GEN MSS 1209) Jean Genet papers relating to George Jackson, Angela Davis, and other Black American activists, circa 1970-1974. (GEN MSS 1820) Jean Genet and Paul Morihien papers relating to publishing, 1947. (GEN MSS Vol 804) Jean Genet page proof and first edition of Journal du voleur, circa 1947-1948. (GEN MSS 1617) Tonnerre de Brest : manuscript. (GEN MSS VOL 801) The Collection of Jean Genet drafts and proofs, 1952-1982. (GEN MSS 1739)
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection and/or revisions in arrangement and description.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Jean Genet papers document the creative process, literary career, and involvement in political and social causes of French author and political activist Jean Genet. The collection contains manuscript material of published and unpublished literary works—including screenplays and essays—alongside political writings, correspondence, personal papers, and other documents related to Genet. The collection spans the years 1940-1988, with the majority of materials dating from 1944 to 1983.

Works form the major portion of the collection. This series includes notes, drafts, revisions, and final versions of Genet's novels, plays, essays, film scripts, and other works. It also contains drafts of political essays and appeals in support of Black American causes.

Genet began writing novels during his periods of incarceration, and these works form a vital part of his literary legacy. Of particular interest is a manuscript of Genet's first novel, Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs , custom bound in a volume with a matching case created by bookbinder Renaud Vernier. Materials related to Pompes Funèbres include the two earliest versions of the novel, written around 1944-1945, that belonged to publishers Paul Morihien and Marc Barbezat, as well as a third group of autograph fragments owned by collector Jacques Guérin.

Genet's drama is extensively represented in the collection, with works such as Les Bonnes , Splendid's , Le Bagne , Le Balcon , Les Nègres , and Les Paravents . The material includes multiple drafts and revised copies of Genet's plays, documenting the evolution of individual works.

Genet's writings for film include an original typescript of Divine , a film adaptation of Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs to be performed by David Bowie. The typescript comes from the papers of Christopher Stamp, who commissioned the screenplay in 1975. The collection also includes material related to Le Langage de la Muraille , a TV series commissioned by Antenne 2 in 1981, to be produced by Danièle Delorme and directed by Bertrand Tavernier. This group includes two sets of manuscripts and corrected typescripts, as well as extensive research files containing a compilation of 19th-century writings on prison reform in France.

Genet's political writings include documents from 1970-1974, supporting George Jackson, Angela Davis, and other Black American activists. The file contains statements denouncing state repression against Black Americans and calling for the release of the Soledad Brothers—George Jackson, John Clutchette, and Fleeta Drumgo. It includes some 300 appeals cards signed by French intellectuals, and drafts of the preface to "L'Assassinat de George Jackson" (Gallimard, November 10, 1971), written after the assassination of prison activist George Jackson by the police. There is also material relating to Angela Davis and the activities mounted by defense committees to demand that Davis be acquitted of all charges against her.

A significant portion of Correspondence and collected materials documents Genet's relationships with Paul Morihien and Marc Barbezat, early publishers of his works. It includes letters, contracts, and other papers related to publishing projects, which often relay Genet's squabbles and monetary demands. Some later materials, from the period 1982-1983, pertain to Genet's involvement with social and political causes, including notes and letters following the publication of his article Quatre heures à Sabra et Chatila (January 1983), as well as his contribution to the exhibition La Rupture (March 1983), which examined the experiences of immigrant workers in France.
Personal papers include photographs from the 1950s and administrative documents related to Genet's residence on Rue des Acacias in Paris (1981-1982).

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Jean Genet Papers are the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

corpname.part: Colonie agricole et pénitentiaire de Mettray
Société nationale de télévision en couleur "Antenne 2"

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Titles of individual works are ordered chronologically by their (approximate) date of composition. Publication or production dates are indicated in parentheses. Archival materials are organized chronologically under each title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains various materials associated with individual works, encompassing literary and political writings. The material includes notes, notebooks, research materials, drafts manuscript, corrected typescripts, page layouts, cover art, page proofs, as well as correspondence and contracts.

The series is organized to reflect Genet's creative output throughout his lifetime. Materials are organized chronologically under each title, with individual works ordered by their date of composition. Publication or production dates are indicated in parentheses.

Works represented in the collection include the novels Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs (Our Lady of the Flowers) (1943), Le Miracle de la Rose (The Miracle of the Rose) (1944), Pompes funèbres (Funeral Rites) (1947), Querelle de Brest (Querelle of Brest) (1947), Journal du voleur (The Thief's Journal) (1949), and the plays Les Bonnes (The Maids) (1947), Spendid's (1993), Le Bagne (The Penal Colony) (1994), Le Balcon (The Balcony) (1956), Les Nègres (The Blacks) (1959), and Les Paravents (The Screens) (1961). Additionally, various notes by the author, in the form of comments or prefaces to his plays, can be found in the Barbezat papers in Series II - Correspondence and collected materials.

This series includes film-related materials, such as drafts of Le Bagne (The Penal Colony) (unproduced, 1950s), Divine (unproduced, 1975), the script for Tony Richardson's Mademoiselle (1966) (an adaptation of Genet's Les Rêves interdits or L'autre versant du rêve, circa 1952), and research documents, manuscripts and typescripts, and contracts for Le Langage de la muraille: cent ans jour après jour (The Language of the Walls: One Hundred Years Day after Day) (1981).

Political writings include material related to Genet's manifesto "Pour George Jackson" (July 1971), appeal cards signed by French intellectuals in support Jackson's release, and drafts of the preface to L'assasinat de George Jackson, published in L'Intolérable 3, a booklet by the Groupe d'Information sur les Prisons (GIP) (Gallimard, 10 November 1971). Additional files relate to activities mounted by defense committees and articles in support of Angela Davis and other Black American activists.

Contextual information supplied with the collection is incorporated into the descriptions of the materials, particularly in the Biography/History notes. These notes provide insights into the various versions and stages of Genet's works and are informed by the research of Michel Corvin, Albert Dichy, Emmanuelle Lambert, and Gilles Philippe (editors of the Pléiade editions), Marguerite Vappereau (film scholar who has studied Genet's film scripts), and others.

bioghist.head: Renaud Vernier (1950-)
bioghist.p: Renaud Vernier (1950-). French bookbinder recognized for his mastery in artisan and artistic bookbinding. His works are characterized by innovative designs and detailed craftsmanship. He blends traditional techniques with contemporary aesthetics, using leather, but also acrylic glass, stainless steel, other metals, and rare woods, among others. Vernier has collaborated with artists and authors to produce unique bindings that often serve as works of art.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs ( Our Lady of the Flowers ) is the debut novel of Jean Genet, written in prison, and first published anonymously at the end of 1943 by Robert Denoël and Paul Morihien. Marc Barbezat/L'Arbalète, published the book in 1948. Genet revised the novel when it was published by Gallimard in 1951; the Gallimard edition omits some of the more pornographic passages in the novel. Those passages were reinstated in the 2021 edition of Oeuvres Complètes published by Gallimard.

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: This is likely a clean copy prepared for the second edition by Barbezat, published in 1948, as it includes most of the stylistic changes made to the original 1944 version. For instance, numerous conjunctions such as "and," "but," "for," "then," etc., are omitted, though a few of these prepositions remain and would later disappear entirely in the final printed text of August 1948.

There are some other variations, such as the inversion of certain words, the use of parentheses, different line breaks, or the use of the pronouns "he" (il) and "she" (elle) to describe the character Divine.

Additionally, the use of the first person is evident in the passage describing the temptation of death haunting young Culafroy [Genet's implied double], which was ultimately rewritten in the third person: "But one day, within my [his] reach, there would surely be a vial of poison that I [he] would only have to bring to my [his] lips" [pp. 36-37 of the manuscript and p. 38, Pléiade edition].

Among the manuscript's revisions, there is an overwriting before Divine's mother's name on the first page, and a sentence crossed out here would later reappear in Barbezat's edition: "J'oublie l'e d'aveugle, j'ai écrit 'aveugl'» (I forget the 'e' in aveugle (blind), I wrote 'aveugl.')

Additionally, this version includes passages that were removed in the 1951 edition of the OEuvres complètes , such as the physical measurements of Seck Gorgui [ff. 81-82], whose name, is spelled "Sek" in this manuscript.
This manuscript of Jean Genet's first novel has been in a private collection until its acquisition by the Beinecke Library in 2023.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Gifted to Michel Papatakis and inscribed by Jean Genet, "Pour Michel Papatakis"; dates unknown. Formerly owned by private collector, France; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Titled and signed at the top:
79 quarto pages (lined paper), with the first 7 pages numbered in Genet's handwriting from 12 to 20, and 13 octavo pages (grid paper), written in black ink, mounted on tabs in a quarto volume.

The manuscript is bound in flexible binding in two-tone citron and brown morocco, housed in a two-tone morocco box with an ebony wood round clasp, slipcase, and protective case, created by Renaud Vernier, 1998.

The manuscript was titled and signed in blue ink at a later date, in the margin of the first page: "Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs Jean Genet". Also inscribed on the first page, in blue ink, "Pour Michel Papatakis. Mon cher Michel, permets-moi de te faire cadeau de ce petit manuscrit de Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs ... Bien amicalement. Jean Genet"

The octavo pages include additions meant to be integrated into the main text, such as the "Divinariane" assembled for the reader's benefit [p. 40]. Please note that the addition found on pages [74-75] was mistakenly bound in this location and contains a few lines meant to be inserted into an earlier passage.

did.unittitle: Le Miracle de la rose , typescript with autograph corrections

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Le Miracle de la Rose is Genet's second novel. He begun writing in early 1943, continued during his incarcerations at the Santé prison and at the Tourelles internment camp, and completed the novel in the spring of 1944, in the months following his release. The book was published by L'Arbalète and printed on March 30, 1946, in a run of 475 copies reserved for subscribers only, a year after the first excerpts appeared in the journal L'Arbalète (no 10, May 1945).

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: One difference between this set and the final version is the absence of songs composed by the inmates of Fontevraud (or Fontevrault according to the author's spelling). There are also small variations: a sentence indicated in brackets or a word underlined, will remain.

The state of this version between manuscript and typescript has been described by Emmanuelle Lambert and Gilles Philippe in the Pléiade edition of Jean Genet, Romans et Poèmes, 2021, as « the copy of the novel that Genet had delivered to Paul Morihien, the first publisher initially considered. This copy presents a complete version of the story in its final state."

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Paul Morihien; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Pierre Bergé; year of acquisition unknown; sold at auction: Pierre Bergé & associés, La Bibliothèque de Pierre Bergé – Sixième vente , Paris, 6 July 2022, lot 1371. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript with 133 pages or slips of autograph corrections, and additional documents, mounted on tabs in a folio volume. Bound in natural linen boards with matching slipcase.
The volume includes :

- Autograph letter signed to Paul Morihien, sending him "half of the manuscript. [...] do not tell Denoël that I gave it to you. You'll get the rest later." He also announces some photos of Mettray and Fontevraud. On the back, written in pencil, the instructions for the notes and corrections added to the typescript. On March 1, 1943, Genet had signed a contract with Paul Morihien, associated with Robert Denoël, for the delivery of three novels: Notre-Dame des Fleurs , le Journal du voleur and Les Enfants du malheur (provisional title of Le Miracle de la rose ), but he eventually entrusted Barbezat with the publication of Le Miracle de la rose .
- Typed title sheet, with the mention "première partie" crossed out and another mention redacted (illegible), dated 1943-44.
- Print of the portrait of Genet by Brassaï (1948), mounted on cardboard (28,5 x 20 cm).

- False-title sheet with the same date of composition (date preceded by a redacted mention), and a dedication to Guy N. (Lucien known as Guy Noppé, Genet's cellmate at the Santé prison and who had been sent a few months after Genet's departure to the Mettray prison. He was one of the models for the character of Bulkaen).
The typescript consists of:
- 261 typed pages (of three different typesets), with multiple numbering: [1]-32, 1-64, [65-84], 1-145.

- 35 autograph pages, 10 of which with additions written on the back, on small in-4 schoolbook leaves, numbered 47-85 (clean-up of the text corresponding to pages 325-400 of the Pléiade edition, 2021).
- 98 autograph additions: inserts ('becquets')-numbered or lettered on leaves of various sizes.

Of the two hundred and sixty-one typewritten sheets, about thirty bear no corrections. All the others show modifications, some in pencil (ranging from the location of a comma to several lines crossed out or added) also inserts mounted opposite the sheets, and indications where to place the additions. Many of the crossed-out or altered words remain legible.
The typescript belonged to Paul Morihien (the first intended publisher of the novel).

did.unittitle: Le Miracle de la rose , autograph revisions for Gallimard edition (1951)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Unbound copy of Miracle de la rose (Lyon: L'Arbalète, 1946) with autograph manuscript revisions for the first trade edition of Genet's Oeuvres complètes, volume 2 (Paris: Gallimard, [1951]).

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Juan Goytisolo. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Juan Goytisolo, 1974.

did.unittitle: Oeuvres complètes , volume 2, incomplete proofs (Paris: Gallimard, 1951)

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Joseph the Provider on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 1988.

bioghist.head: History of the edition
bioghist.p: Jean Genet. Oeuvres complètes, Tome II , was published in March 1951 by Gallimard. Volume II includes two novels: Notre-Dame des Fleurs ( Our Lady of the Flowers ) and Miracle de la rose ( Miracle of the Rose ), and two poems: Le Condamné à mort ( The Condemned Man ) and Un chant d'amour ( A Song of Love ).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Incomplete proofs of Oeuvres complètes, Volume 2, with half title and running title Oeuvres de Jean Genet, containing autograph manuscript corrections and deletions in black ink and editors' markings in blue pencil.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: La Galère is inscribed "à Nico Dakis", meaning Nico Papatakis. Papatakis met Genet through a mutual friend encountered at the Café de Flore. He led Genet into the burglary of an acquaintance's apartment. They left with a suitcase full of rare books, which they later sold.
The poem was composed by Genet as a counterpart to Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs : it is a tribute to the assassin Harcamone, a name also used for the hero of Miracle of the Rose .
The original printed edition of La Galère , illustrated with six original etchings by Leonor Fini, was published in 1947, printed in 80 copies by Jacques Loyau at the author's expense.
The book was condemned on July 3, 1954, by the correctional court of the Seine, a ruling that was overturned on appeal "for procedural reasons unrelated to the offense itself."

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Gifted to Nico Papatakis and inscribed by Jean Genet, "à Nico Dakis"; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Thierry Maulnier; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A "manuscript edition" of La Galère , in a modern, blue cloth case, with two "Japanese-style" bone clasps on green silk ribbons.

Autograph manuscript on 8 large sheets of heavy paper, folded in half to form a booklet of 16 pages. Text written only on the recto: the first page bears the title, the second the dedication "A Nico Dakis", the next fourteen contain the text of the poem, and a final blank page. The manuscript includes fifteen corrections and one addition. Jean Genet claims to have copied his poem in fifteen copies. Each copy contained a variant.

Accompanied by a typed page describing the manuscript, the history of the work, and provenance of the object ("Ce manuscript provient de la collection de Thierry Maulnier auquel il avait été offert"). Likely a text provided by a dealer during a previous public auction or private sale.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The material consists of early drafts and autograph fragments of Pompes funèbres and comes from the collections of Paul Morihien, Marc Barbezat, and Jacques Guérin.

did.unittitle: "Le Spectre du coeur", typescript with corrections

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: In a letter addressed to Marc Barbezat, Genet evokes the novel under its primitive title as early as November 1943 and reports the involuntary destruction of his manuscript. He resumed writing in March 1944, but his work was disrupted by the death of his lover Jean Decarnin, who was killed by a militiaman the following August, during the liberation of Paris.
Jean Genet, in need of money, took his manuscript to Gallimard who printed a first clandestine edition in 1947 under the definitive title of Pompes funèbres .
In 1948, Paul Morihien publishes a second, non-commercial edition.

The novel appears for the first time in bookstores in Genet's OEuvres complètes , Volume III (Complete Works), published by Gallimard in 1953. ( Genet, Romans et Poèmes , Gallimard, La Pléiade, 2021, pp. 1456-1461)

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: The typescript comes from the archives of Paul Morihien—to whom Genet had first ceded the rights to the novel—and has been identified by Emmanuelle Lambert and Gilles Philippe, editors of the Pléiade, as the oldest version available to date, based on the typing, the corrections of the typescripts and later manuscripts, some of which bear other titles ( L'OEil de Gabès or La Vie inférieure ), and on the organization of the text itself.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Paul Morihien; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Pierre Bergé; year of acquisition unknown; sold at auction: Pierre Bergé & associés, La Bibliothèque de Pierre Bergé – Sixième vente, Paris, 6 July 2022, lot 1372. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript corrected by Jean Genet and autograph additions, mounted on tabs. Folio volume bound in natural linen boards with matching linen slipcase.
The volume contains:

- 205 typed pages numbered from 1 to 204 (page 46 is missing, page [49] is not numbered, one page 79bis, pages 94bis and ter correspond to pages 95 and 96, pages 144 to 149 have been placed after page 171bis and renumbered 171 ter to 8). The corrections, in blue or black ink, are sometimes on a single word, sometimes on several lines with autograph additions. Some leaves have been cut out and reassembled.
- 99 autograph additions, numbered or lettered, on sheets of various sizes, mostly on grid paper, one of which has working notes on the back.
- An original drawing by Jean Cocteau for a draft cover, in black ink, on the verso of a sheet with the address of the Maison du Bailli, in Milly-la-Forêt [Cocteau's property acquired in 1947]
- An autograph title sheet signed on pink cardstock bearing: Le Spectre du coeur / Jean Genet.
- 1 page autograph letter, signed, undated, addressed to Paul Morihien.
Mon petit Paul
La phrase est ce que je l'ai écrite. Ni plus ni moins. C'est bien davide. Qu'on n 'y change rien.
Je sais ce que j'écris et que je t'embrasse
Jean
(My dear Paul / The sentence is exactly as I wrote it. No more, no less. It's really davide. Let nothing be changed. / I know what I write and that I kiss you / Jean)
- Four impressions on 'papier de Chine' of compositions by Jean Cocteau, illustrations from his Livre blanc .

did.unittitle: "La vie inférieure", autograph manuscript and typescript with corrections

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: The collection comes from the archives of Marc Barbezat.

The manuscript lacks parts and chapters but is close to the published version. It contains just over half of the text. It is, with the manuscript owned by Paul Morihien, the only known manuscripts of the novel. Emmanuelle Lambert, editor of the Pléiade edition, was unable to determine the writing order of the two manuscripts.

The typescript, heavily corrected and supplemented with autograph additions, does not continue the manuscript. "Close to its final version, though divided into parts and chapters, the text is incomplete. But it contains some passages missing from all the other sets" ( Genet, Romans et Poèmes , Bibliothèque de la Pléiade, 2021, p. 1459). It is likely a draft used for the final clean-up of the text.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2024.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript and corrected typescript mounted on tabs and bound in black morocco with accompanying slipcase by Alix.
The volume consists of:
- 92 autograph sheets from a school notebook.
- a corrected typescript of 194 sheets.
- a cardboard folder with the title La Vie inférieure in Genet's hand, a list of characters on the first page, and Le Spectre du cœur inscribed on the third page.

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: Working manuscripts with deletions and corrections, representative of Genet's method; he would write a few lines or entire passages on fragments of sheets, sometimes grouping them by gluing them together, and then adding them to the rest of his manuscript.
These fragments mostly relate to the description of the relationships between the narrator, Erik, and Paulo. Some sheets follow one another, such as those numbered in blue, from 336 to 349.
Two sheets present unpublished passages (numbered 466 and 467), each about fifteen lines long. The first depicts Paulo imagining himself as a 'Gypsy', and the second describes Paulo dreaming.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Jacques Guérin; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Consist of 32 pages of various sizes and on different papers, in pencil and ink, numbered in blue pencil. Also a kraft paper envelope titled in the hand of Jacques Guérin: « Pompes Funèbres / Morceaux manuscrits à relier avec le volume imprimé » ("Pompes Funèbres / Manuscript pieces to be bound with the printed volume") and a half-black morocco folder by Devauchelle.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Jacques Guérin; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; year of acquisition unknown; sold at auction: Pierre Bergé & associés, Collection Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn sale, Paris, 7 November 2019, lot 237. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2019.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First draft for the published title Querelle de Brest (Éditions Paul Morihien, 1947). Signed by Jean Genet, titled Tonnerre de Brest , dated 1945. The novel's story centers on Georges Querelle, a bisexual sailor and drug dealer in the port town of Brest, France. Written in a notebook with additional pages inserted throughout, the draft is divided into 32 chapters and includes sections not retained in the published text. Includes a presentation inscription from Genet to industrialist and philanthropist Jacques Guérin, who acquired the manuscript in 1947.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group consists of three autograph manuscripts related to Les Bonnes :
- 'Pour Les Bonnes' ('For The Maids'). Autograph manuscript [1947?]. ½ page, in black ink on lined paper. Stage directions and interpretation notes. "J'ai peu de choses à dire. Voici quelques notes…"
- Letter to Jean-Jacques Pauvert. Autograph manuscript signed "J. Genet." [1954]. 4 pages, in black ink on lined paper, small tears predating the writing.

Complete text of the letter-preface accompanying the publication of the two versions of Les Bonnes ( The Maids ) by Jean-Jacques Pauvert in 1954, in which Genet reflects on the writing of his play and explains what he would expect from a play that is more than mere entertainment:

« Mon cher Pauvert. Il vous faut donc une présentation. Mais que dire d'une pièce dont j'étais détaché avant même qu'elle fût achevée ? Parler de sa composition serait évoquer un monde et un climat sans grandeur….. » ("My dear Pauvert, So you need an introduction. But what is there to say about a play from which I had detached myself even before it was finished? To speak of its composition would be to evoke a world and a climate devoid of grandeur….")
- 'Comment jouer Les Bonnes' ('How to Perform The Maids'). Autograph manuscript [1961?]. 3 pages, in blue ink, with some corrections.

Text published as the preface to the reissue of Les Bonnes
(The Maids) , by Marc Barbezat/l'Arbalète in 1963. « Furtif. C'est le mot qui s'impose d'abord. Le jeu théâtral des deux actrices figurant les deux bonnes doit être furtif… » ("Furtive. That's the word that comes to mind first...")

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Journal du voleur is a semi-autobiographical novel, first published by Skira in the fall of 1948. It was subsequently published by Gallimard in July 1949 with a text censored by Genet himself; the original text was republished in 2021 Pléiade edition.
Excerpts were published in Les Temps Modernes in July-August 1946.

did.unittitle: Journal du voleur , two manuscript fragments

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscripts of two long passages from Journal du voleur ( The Thief's Journal ). 24 pages of drafts in blue ink on school notebook paper, lined. Contain some crossings-out and corrections but are close to the 1948 edition, except for a few punctuation variations.
First fragment is 13 pages, numbered 1-12 by Genet, and corresponds to the first meeting of the narrator with Lucien.
Second fragment is 11 pages, numbered 13-21 by Genet, and corresponds to the narrator's relationship with Inspector Bernardini.

did.unittitle: Journal du voleur , page proofs of the first edition

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Jacques Guérin; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; year of acquisition unknown; sold at auction: Pierre Bergé & associés, Collection Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn sale, Paris, 7 November 2019, lot 249. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2019.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Page proofs with autograph manuscript corrections, for publication by Skira in 1948, and autograph manuscript markings identifying expurgated sections for publication by Gallimard in 1949. Accompanied by a kraft paper envelope with an autograph inscription in red and blue reading "Genet/Journal du voleur/épreuves"; and a manuscript note regarding the revised proofs, which reads "1ères épreuves corrigées par J.G. Elles contiennent de nombreux passages supprimés de sa main par l'auteur comme poussés trop loin par sa passion érotique" ("First proofs corrected by J.G. They contain numerous passages deleted by the author's hand, deemed to have gone too far in their erotic passion."), possibly in the hand of collector Jacques Guérin.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Jacques Guérin; dates unknown. Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; year of acquisition unknown; sold at auction: Pierre Bergé & associés, Collection Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn sale, Paris, 7 November 2019, lot 250. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2019.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A printed copy from the original edition, clandestinely published by Albert Skira, with a print run of 410 copies 'hors commerce' ("not for sale"). Number 7 of the first 10 copies "sur velin teinté à la forme des papeteries d'Arches" (on tinted Velin d'Arches paper), signed by Genet, and with an inscription from Genet to "Monsieur Girard," apparently Georges Girard, a printer at Skira, undated.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: The play Splendid's was announced for the stage several times during the 1950s, but Genet abandoned it, and it was only published posthumously in 1993, based on the typescripts preserved by Marc Barbezat. It premiered in 1993 at the Théâtre des Amandiers in Nanterre, directed by Stanislas Nordey.

This play is inspired by a real-life incident mentioned in Le journal du voleur
(The Thief's Journal) : the kidnapping of a millionaire's daughter, who was killed by a gang of criminals hiding on the top floor of a luxury hotel. As often happened, the title underwent several changes, with Genet hesitating between Frolic's, Le Bagne, Santé Forlano, and La Rafale, the nickname of one of the kidnappers.

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: The 3 manuscripts include corrections, deletions, and additions, characteristic of Genet's working method, and represent three early versions of what would become Splendid's , for which Genet signed a contract in 1948 to have it published in the magazine L'Arbalète in 1949.

These fragments have been studied in the Pléiade edition of Jean Genet. Théâtre complet , 2002 (p. 1096 and following), which notes their public auction appearances, including at Drouot on December 16, 1987, and Sotheby's London on May 26, 1994.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat; dates unknown. Sold at auction: Drouot, Paris, 16 December 1987; Sotheby's, London, 26 May 1994; Drouot, Paris, 30 November 1995. Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group consists of three autograph manuscripts of the play Splendid's (published posthumously in 1993).

- The first manuscript features a signed title page, with a crossed-out title: "Leur toupet était déjà célèbre / Play in 2 acts / Jean Genet" (40 pages and an addition on a half-page and on the verso of one sheet, written on grid-patterned school paper in black, brown, or blue ink). The first page includes an original ink drawing depicting the set design for the play ("The corridors of the top floor of a palace. Very sumptuous...") and introduces the characters, who still bear foreign-sounding names.

- The second manuscript begins with a signed title page: "Leur toupet était célèbre / Act 1 / Jean Genet," with three lines of dialogue written diagonally (37 pages and three half-pages, numbered 1bis to 14, then 2 to 24, 30 to 38, and 42 to 44, written in blue ballpoint pen on school notebook pages).
- The third manuscript consists of 18 pages (numbered and cut into various formats, written in blue ballpoint pen on school notebook pages), presenting a highly fragmented version of the text.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group consists of the first edition of L'Enfant criminel & 'Adame miroir (1949), autograph manuscripts, correspondence with Paul Morihien and a contract. In a blue folder and clamshell box, illustrated with a reproduction of Genet's portrait taken in Mettray in 1927.

did.unittitle: L'Enfant criminel & 'Adame miroir , first edition (Paul Morihien, 1949)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First edition, paperback, cover illustrated with a black-and-white photographic reproduction, edition band retained, unopened. Published by Paul Morihien, February 1949. From a print run of 50 copies on Marais Crève-Cœur paper; copy numbered "HC 2/4" ('hors commerce' "not for sale"). In a blue folder and clamshell box, illustrated with a reproduction of a portrait of Genet taken at Mettray in 1927.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Duplicate of the contract from Éditions Morihien, signed by Genet and Paul Morihien in February 1949 for L'Enfant criminel .

The date, typed, has been corrected from the 1st to the 28th of February. It includes a correction by Genet regarding the title, initially 'L'Enfance criminelle', and three marginal notes, one signed and in Genet's handwriting. Jean Genet assigns the rights to print and sell his book for a flat sum of 50,000 francs, under certain conditions regarding translation and reprints. He listed his address in Paris, Hôtel de la Fleur de Lys.

did.unittitle: Jean Genet to Paul Morihien, autograph letters

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 autograph letters addressed to Paul Morihien concerning the money Genet is owed, and the printing and rights to his story.

- In the first letter Genet informs Morihien he is sending the proofs and final approval for printing. He explains he is in hiding, to avoid the police who came to his hotel. He asks Paul Morihien to give him 20,000 francs of the money he is owed for L'Enfant criminel , which Jean [Cocteau?] will bring to him. 1 page oblong octavo on grid paper.

- In the second letter, Genet protests against Morihien's decision to reduce his fee to 30,000 francs. He threatens to have the publication of his books legally stopped. He has been sentenced to two years in prison, the police are looking for him, and he accuses Morihien to have taken advantage of him and his situation.

- In the third letter, after the publication and success of the pamphlet, Genet demands an additional 30,000 francs owed from his percentage, in addition to the 50,000 francs already obtained. He provides the address of Marius Sénémaud, in Cannes, to send the money. 1 page quarto on grid paper.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1 sheet (2 pages, including crossed out text on verso that reads "Le texte qu'on va lire était fait pour être entendu. Il sera publié néanmoins. Toutefois j'ai peu d'espoir que le lisent les enfants à qui je le destinais." ("The text we are about to read was meant to be broadcast. It will be published. However, I have little hope it will be read by the children I intended to reach.")

Genet had been invited to participate in the radio program "Carte Blanche" by Fernand Pouey. Genet planned to broadcast L'Enfant criminel but was ultimately banned from speaking on air. He resorted to publishing the text with a preface explaining the circumstances around the publication.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Le Bagne (The Penal Colony) is a script for a film and a play, set in a prison modeled after the penal colony of Cayenne in French Guiana. The story centers on carceral life and power relationships between the convicts. Le Bagne was posthumously published in 1994 by L'Arbalète, Marc Barbezat's publishing house.

For about fifteen years, from 1949 to 1964, Genet wrote thousands of pages on the penal colony—a place with personal meaning that he explored in prose poem, film screenplay, and theatrical script. The screenplay for Le Bagne was likely written between 1952-1954 and was left aside in 1956, before being produced. Genet started writing the theatrical play in 1958 and destroyed the final version at the time of the suicide of his partner Abdallah Bentaga in March 1964. However, according to Albert Dichy in Théatre Complet , two sets of drafts remain: one the author gave to his publisher, Marc Barbezat, in 1963, and another given to his literary agent and American translator, Bernard Frechtman; Marc Barbezat acquired the Frechtman set at auction on October 31, 1990, and used both sets of documents to create the posthumous version of the play published in 1994. Using these groups of material, Michel Corvin and Albert Dichy published a different version in Théatre Complet in 2002. The 1990 auction catalogue from Drouot describes the manuscripts owned by Frechtman as containing 163 pages paginated 1-162, which suggests the autograph manuscripts of the play held at the Beinecke originate from the Frechtman Collection.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat. Sold at auction: Drouot, Collection Marc Barbezat. Jean Genet , Paris, 5 March 1999. Formerly owned by private collector. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group consists of a completed film screenplay, approximately 1952-1954, and fragments of a play script, between 1958-1963, for Le Bagne .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Heavily corrected carbon copy.

did.unittitle: Le Bagne , autograph manuscript fragments of the theatrical play

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Bernard Frechtman. Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat who purchased the manuscript at auction on October 31, 1990.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written in blue and turquoise ink, black and blue ball-point pens, on at least six different papers; includes drawings published by L'Arbalète in its 1994 posthumous edition.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2021 and 2023.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Le Balcon (The Balcony) is set in an unnamed city during a revolutionary uprising. The action takes place in a brothel that reflects the social order under threat outside it.

Le Balcon (The Balcony) exists in three distinct versions, published in French in 1956, 1960, and 1962 by Marc Barbezat/L'Arbalète. Genet wrote the first version of the play between January and September 1955, during which time he also wrote Les Nègres (The Blacks) and re-worked his screenplay Le Bagne (The Penal Colony).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group contains drafts, manuscripts, typescripts, and proofs of the first and second editions of Le Balcon published respectively in 1956 and 1960 by L'Arbalète, as well as material related to the translated editions of Deathwatch and The Balcony published in 1961 and 1966 by Faber and Faber.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group contains drafts, autograph manuscripts, typescripts, and corrected proofs, 1955-1956, for the first edition of Le Balcon (June 1956), with substantive revisions and corrections by Genet. Also includes two hand-colored lithographs by Alberto Giacometti, created as a cover illustration for the book.
French titles transcribed from inscriptions on wrappers by Marc Barbezat.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: First autograph manuscript of Le Balcon . In two notebooks with additional laid-in sheets.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat. Sold at auction: Drouot, Collection Marc Barbezat. Jean Genet, Paris, 5 March 1999. Formerly owned by private collector. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript in Les Goélands notebook (a typical French student's cahier) and laid-in pages. Notebook pages numbered 1, 13-25, 39-86, and laid-in pages numbered 2-10 and 26-34

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript in brick-colored notebook with pages numbered 1-24 (starting with "7e Tableau"), and additional laid-in pages numbered 25-40 (starting with "12e Tableau").

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Yellow wrapper inscribed by Marc Barbezat: "le Balcon. 1ère version. Manuscrit novembre 1955".

custodhist.head: Custodial History

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Second version consisting of typescript with autograph corrections in green ink. On the folder cover, Genet's autograph accounting notes, with sums in French francs and in Italian lira.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat. Sold at auction: Drouot, Collection Marc Barbezat. Jean Genet, Paris, 5 March 1999. Formerly owned by private collector. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Third version consisting of annotated typescript and autograph pages for typesetting.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Annotated typescript, in green ink, pages numbered 1-97.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Annotated typescript, in green ink, pages numbered 97-141.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph additions of pages rewritten by the author, in green ink: "pages refaites, 3ème version," numbered 42-49, 54-57, 63-67, 73-77, 83-91.

did.unittitle: "4ème version sur épreuves à imprimerie, mars 1956"

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat. Sold at auction: Drouot, Collection Marc Barbezat. Jean Genet, Paris, 5 March 1999. Formerly owned by private collector. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fourth version consisting of galley proofs with autograph notes.

Cover inscription: "Tirade des putains dans le livre jaune, scène du juge, discours des jeunes gens, acceptation de Madame Irma, Entre le plombier et Chantal, dès le début, la scène d'amour qui se continue en 2e partie" [translation: Tirade of whores in the yellow book, scene of the judge, speech of young people, acceptance of Madame Irma, Between the plumber and Chantal, from the beginning, the love scene which continues in part 2] as well as autograph additions and corrections on loose, numbered pages.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Corrected galley proofs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph additions and extensive corrections on stage directions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fifth and final version to the edition of Le Balcon published in June 1956 by L'Arbalète. Blue-grey wrapper inscribed by Barbezat, who indicates the 5th version contains final proofs and original lithographs by Giacometti, colored in pencil by the artist.
Wrapper signed by Jean Genet and inscribed with "bon à tirer".

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat. Sold at auction: Drouot, Collection Marc Barbezat. Jean Genet, Paris, 5 March 1999. Formerly owned by private collector. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2021.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Final corrected proofs.
Includes two original lithographs with color pencil drawings by Alberto Giacometti, printed by Atelier Mourlot, Paris. One lithograph was used for the book cover, the other is a variant.

custodhist.head: Custodial History

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
