scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph page with on top the unfinished sentence "Un préfet de police sait qu'au bordel une", followed by « Cette pièce a pour sujet les mythologies du bordel. Un préfet de police est affolé, navré, chagrin de constater qu'au 'Grand Balcon' sont représentés de nombreux rites érotiques ayant pour héros : l'abbé, le héros, le criminel, le mendiant - d'autres encore - hélas jamais le préfet de police. Il tâchera de faire que son propre personnage enfin, par une grâce exquise, hante les rêveries érotiques, et devienne ainsi héros de la mythologie du Bordel ». At the bottom of the page "Prière d'insérer pour [?] / le 1er juin 1956 à Décines"

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printed proofs (on alfa paper) for Le Balcon [L'Arbalète, 1956]. Text of the first two scenes, with a copyright page sheet containing handwritten corrections on the publication dates of Genet's works.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group contains autograph manuscripts, typescripts, corrected proofs, and a maquette cover, for the second edition of Le Balcon (1960).

did.unittitle: Corrected typecript for the second edition

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 143 pages, with autograph corrections by Genet and annotations in another hand.

First draft of the typescript of the version intended for the second edition, in 9 scenes instead of 15, published in 1960 by Barbezat. Includes a handwritten note specifying that the pages crossed out in black are not to be set in type, as the galleys already exist, and only the passages circled in red are to be set in type. It adds that the text crossed out in blue "est supprimé par Genet" ("was removed by Genet").

This draft contains around twenty autograph corrections and additions, sometimes affecting several lines, and on the verso of certain pages. In addition to these author annotations, there are a few corrections in another hand.
This version includes Scene VI, which does not appear in the original edition and which will not be included in editions after 1960 (but was published in Théâtre complet , Pléiade, 2002, p. 423).

The typescript is accompanied by a note in Barbezat's hand suggesting corrections and improvements to Genet, particularly concerning the 6th scene. Genet responded in blue ink to some of these suggestions, explaining or justifying his work.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Corrected proofs with two autograph annotations and corrections in another hand [possibly Frechtman's?].

Complete text with title and list of characters, along with 2 sheets on different paper—one repeating the text and the other specifying that the dedication to the actor Pierre Joly [considered for a role in Le Bagne] should be removed. The corrections, in black ink, mainly address typographical and layout errors. With a note in pencil: "Le Balcon d'après copie dactylographiée – 1er jeu" ("Le Balcon based on the typed copy / 1st set"). Genet's annotations concern the title and the list of characters.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript signed 'J. G.' of the foreword ("Avertissement"), November 1959, which was first published in the 1960 edition, and replaced by "Comment jouer le Balcon" ("How to perform the Balcony") in the 1968 edition. Text on two sheets of lined paper, written on recto in blue ink. « La représentation fictive d'une action, d'une expérience, nous dispense généralement de tenter de les accomplir sur le plan réel et en nous-même. […] Voilà ce qu'une conscience conciliante ne cesse de souffler aux spectateurs. Or, aucun problème exposé ne devrait être résolu dans l'imaginaire surtout que la solution dramatique s'empresse vers un ordre social achevé. Au contraire, que le mal, sur la scène, explose, nous montre nus, nous laisse hagards s'il se peut et n'ayant de recours qu'en nous »… (The fictitious representation of an action, of an experience, generally spares us the effort of attempting to carry them out in reality and within ourselves"...)

Corrected proof of the foreword ("Avertissement"), in blue ink, with annotations on the verso. Genet requests that the proof be sent back to him, and on the verso, he adds that it should be returned to Frechtman 'who will send it back to me.' In a different handwriting and ink: « Pour les Nègres : mettez le nom des personnages et en face ou en dessous le nom des comédiens / Date de la création / Théâtre de Lutèce. Paris. Merci. Jean ». (For Les Nègres: put the names of the characters and next to or below them the names of the actors / Date of the premiere / Théâtre de Lutèce. Paris. Thank you. Jean.) Les Nègres, published in January 1958, was premiered by Roger Blin in October 1959.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fragment on blue-green card stock paper. On recto: titles of plays (published or not), on verso, partial sketch of a square with "Premier tableau" ("First scene") written on it.
Maquette for the cover of Le Balcon , published by L'Arbalète in 1960.

did.unittitle: "Comment jouer Le Balcon " ("How To Perform The Balcony ")

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

did.unittitle: "Notes pour jouer le Balcon", autograph manuscript

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 4 sheets written on recto, in blue ink. Text published in the preface of the 1968 edition under the title « Comment jouer Le Balcon » ("How to Perform The Balcony") and included in the Oeuvres complètes , volume IV. Genet cites certain staging examples, pointing out what should not be done, and reasserting his intent as the author. The text is final and nearly complete, missing only the last paragraph, thus ending with the words: «Tout ce que je viens d'écrire ne s'adresse pas bien sûr à un metteur en scène intelligent. Il sait ce qu'il a à faire. Mais les autres ? » ("Everything I've just written is, of course, not directed at an intelligent director. He knows what to do. But the others?").

Also, proofs of about twenty sheets corresponding to the 1968 Gallimard edition: "Notes pour jouer Le Balcon " ("How to Perform The Balcony ") (incomplete text, with layout instructions in green ink) and a fragment of Scene I (15 sheets).

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

did.unittitle: Proofs of translated editions of Deathwatch and The Balcony

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two sets of corrected proofs by Bernard Frechtman (Genet's agent and translator) for publisher Faber and Faber:

The Balcony ( Le Balcon ). Second English edition by Faber and Faber, 1966, corrected by Frechtman. Includes the nomination certificate in the category 'Best-Written American Drama' by the Writers Guild of America for the Screen Writers' Annual Award of 1963 (1 printed page on parchment paper).

Deathwatch ( Haute-surveillance ). First English edition by Faber and Faber, 1961. The text is based on the 1949 stage version produced at the théâtre des Mathurins rather than the text published by Gallimard that same year (1949).

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2021 and 2023.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Les Nègres (The Blacks) is a theatrical play that explores racial prejudice and stereotypes, designed to be played by black actors donning white masks. Romain Rouleau commissioned the work in the fall 1954, with the first edition printed in January 1958 by l'Arbalète. The first theatrical production was directed by Roger Blin and premiered on October 18, 1959, at the Théatre de Lutèce, Paris, with black actors from "Les Griots" theater company. The cast included Robert Liensol, Lydia Ewande, Toto Bissainthe, Bachir Touré, Darling Légitimus, Mamadou Condé, Gérard Lemoine, Dia Fara, Judith Aucagos, Gisèle Baka, Edée Fortin, Georges Hilarion, and Théo Légitimus, with scenography and costumes by André Acquart.
A revised edition of Les Nègres published in 1960 by l'Arbalète included 33 photographs by Ernst Scheidegger, taken during the rehearsals of the play at the Théatre de Lutèce in 1959.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: The subseries includes material from mixed provenance:

Box 12, Folders 1-5 Box 13, Folders 1-2, 4-5 Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat. Sold at auction: Drouot, Collection Marc Barbezat. Jean Genet, Paris, 5 March 1999. Formerly owned by private collector. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2021.
Box 12, Folder 6-8 Box 13, Folder 3 Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group includes drafts, proofs and drawings for the cover of Les Nègres , 1955-1957.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Second autograph manuscript of the play, written in Venice, Italy, in discrete green and red notebooks, signed and dated March 1955.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript with autograph additions, re-titled "Foot-Ball." The wrapper is inscribed by Barbezat: "Foot-Ball. Manuscrit 2ème version. Mai 1956." The typescript corresponds to the third version of the play written by Genet.

did.unittitle: Les Nègres , preface, manuscript and typescript

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1955, December. Unpublished preface. Two copies including one with autograph corrections.
1956, summer. Autograph manuscript on lined paper and typescript.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed and inscribed by Genet: "Bon pour la composition" (ready for typesetting).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph additions and corrections to be incorporated into a typed version:
- 6 pages with autograph additions, corresponding to pages 17, 23, 43, 56, 114, 139 of the typed version.
- 'Litanie des Blêmes' to replace a paragraph on page 68.
- 10-page manuscript with a scene between Félicité, Bobo, and Village, numbered 72-81 and 1-10 (pp. 505, and 507-514, in the Pléiade edition).
Two corrected typescript fragments: 'Page 17' 'Page 132,' with corrections in ink and pencil (p. 481, Pléiade edition).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 17 autograph sketches for the book cover, two of which bear the phrase « une clownerie de Jean Genet » ("a clownery by Jean Genet"), to appear on the interior title page. All in charcoal on Lana watermark paper. Also included the orange wrapper titled in Genet's hand "Jean Genet Les Nègres / Les Nègres de Jean Genet"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript "Pour jouer les Nègres" (OHow to perform Les Nègres) [1959-1960?] with comments on Roger Blin's direction. 3 pages on grid paper, in blue ink, with a 6-line passage crossed out and not retained ("Once the curtain goes down, it is lifted again…").

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Proof pages numbered 1-94 on grey paper with autograph pages inserted at pages 76 and 84.

did.unittitle: Galley proofs with corrected pages, "6ème version sur mise en page…"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Galley proofs and copies of pages 72-76, with autograph corrections. Signed by Genet on the front page, also inscribed by Marc Barbezat: "6ème version sur mise en page et corrections pour ultimes versions".

did.unittitle: Galley proofs with corrected pages [7ème version?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Set of corrected proofs of the original edition published by Barbezat in January 1958. Proofs on Lana paper (with the exception of 2 sheets), with the half-title page also bearing a request for three new proofs on the same fine paper. 25 pages contain corrections in Genet's hand, both in the text and on the title, dedication, and colophon pages. This set used the justification from Le Balcon (1956), with a few pencil corrections by the typesetter.

The first sheet contains the complete autograph text, signed 'J.G.', of the foreword, with the note: « Préface - mais ne mettez pas ce mot » « Cette pièce, je le répète, écrite par un Blanc est destinée à un public de Blancs... » ("Foreword (but do not use this word)" "This play, I repeat, written by a White man, is intended for a White audience...")
Also, two printed proofs from another set, with autograph corrections, one of which contains a long addition on the verso.
Note: This set of corrected proofs was acquired by the Beinecke Library in 2023, and was interfiled by the archivist between the 6th version and "avant-dernière version".

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Galley proofs with minimal autograph corrections in pencil. Wrapper in Kraft paper inscribed by Genet: "Avant dernier jeu d'épreuves. Les Nègres "

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Galley proofs in hard cover wrapper inscribed by Genet: "Les Nègres. Bon à tirer". Additional page numbered 88 with autograph corrections.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group consists of autograph manuscripts and a copy of the first edition of Les Paravents , 1961.

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: This working manuscript divided in "chapters" will lead to the more comprehensive later versions. With rapid handwriting and few corrections, this draft serves as a sort of outline for the play.
Les Paravents will be typeset by Gallimard at the end of 1958 but actually published, in a revised form, by L'Arbalète in 1961, and directed by Roger Blin at the Théâtre de l'Odéon in 1966.
This draft presents significant differences and variants, detailed in the long notice of the 2002 Gallimard edition (Pléiade, p. 1227-1232).

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript consists of 38 pages, quarto, in blue ink, with a few lined sheets and ½ page, octavo, inscribed with: « Brouillon des derniers chapitres de La Mère » ("Draft of the last chapters of La Mère").
The two sheets numbered "XXII" feature a sketch of "screens" in the upper margin, one labeled "Le bordel" ("the brothel").

It is divided into 12 sections (one of which is unnumbered and spans two sheets), with some numbers repeated, and some sections titled: XXI. La Mère (continued). XXII. Le Bordel (The Brothel). XXIV. La Mère (continued). XXV. Le Village (The Village). XXV. Les Soldats (The Soldiers). XXVI. L'Enfer (Hell). La Mort (Death).

This is a working manuscript from an early version of Les Paravents
(The Screens) , with additions in a different ink.

bioghist.head: Version of the text
bioghist.p: The manuscript begins with stage directions for the set, characters, and actions that will appear in the published edition, but it presents numerous significant variants, with some passages heavily rewritten, others deleted, or reworked.

This is a near-complete version of an unpublished draft (not mentioned in the Pléiade edition), preceding the 1958 Gallimard proofs, which were announced as containing 31 scenes but actually only have 30 (numbered I to XXIX, with a XXVIII bis).

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript. 148 pages with some corrections and additions, on lined paper. In half-morocco black folder with patterned boards, in a matching slipcase.
The title page is inscribed "La Mère. Féérie en 31 tableaux" and signed.

Most of the scenes begin with a page describing only the set, composed of screens or other elements, with 18 sketches (for the first 20, except for scenes XVII and XIX). Some of these drawings have been crossed out by Genet.
The final page, numbered in pencil "148", is inscribed "Fin" and signed.

did.unittitle: "Saïd Epreuves Corrigées" and "Intercalaires des Paravents", manuscript

bioghist.head: Version of the draft
bioghist.p: In this version the structure in tableaux is not yet finalized. The draft was entrusted to a typist with indications for future additions or corrections. It begins with a title page featuring a dialogue evoking the scene where the Lieutenant expresses his wish to see intestines in the sun.
This version will later be reshaped and enriched, both in terms of dialogue and descriptions of the sets.

... Among the collected fragments, some passages directly relate to the known versions of The Screens , particularly scenes involving the characters of the mother, Leila, or the soldiers, as well as notes on the set composed of screens. However, there are also quite different scenes, with character names that will not appear in the final play, such as Pierre, Roger, or Nestor, or a dialogue scene between two "bourgeois" couples that evokes other Genet plays, like The Balcony .

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript signed, partly typewritten, with autograph or typewritten fragments. Most of the typewritten pages have autograph additions or corrections, sometimes on the back of the page. In half-morocco black folders. Consists of two folders.

Folder titled "Saïd / Epreuves corrigées" contains 219 pages, quarto, with some half-sheets and a few on lined paper, about sixty pages fully autograph, including the title page inscribed "Saïd" and signed, all numbered in red ink.
Folder titled "intercalaires des Paravents" contains 87 pages, quarto or octavo, mostly on lined sheets, in black, blue, or red ink, including about thirty typewritten pages. All numbered in pencil.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Presented to Marcelle Ranson and inscribed by Genet "à Marcelle Ranson…". Formerly owned by Pierre Bergé; year of acquisition unknown; sold at auction: Pierre Bergé & associés, La Bibliothèque de Pierre Bergé – Sixième vente, Paris, 6 July 2022, lot 1379. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paperback copy on Bouffant Alfa paper from the first edition of Les Paravents published by Marc Barbezat. In a lemon-colored morocco case with a flat spine and a box by Roger Devauchelle.
A working copy belonging to the actress Marcelle Ranson, the creator of the role of Ommou, includes an autograph inscription, signed:
"à Marcelle Ranson // qui a donné // le sens exact au mot // "défendre", car elle // a défendu les Paravents // Amicalement // Jean Genet"
"To Marcelle Ranson // who gave the exact meaning to the word // 'defend,' because she // defended The Screens // Affectionately // Jean Genet"
The copy is crossed out, corrected, and annotated by several hands, including that of Jean Genet (p. 129) and Marcelle Ranson (in red).

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2023.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 4 pages on letterhead from the Excelsior Gallia Hotel in Milan. The 25 maxims or observations are numbered in Roman numerals.
Excerpt:

I. A man is never worth more than another man, nor ever more than a woman. II. Property should not exceed certain limits. Objects—never possessions—are interchangeable, and thus the temporary holders of these possessions should never be covered with too thick a layer of emotional attachment. III. Every sexual perversion is not merely encouraged: it is recommended. If every sexual perversion flourishes as it pleases—whether in freedom or in a consented and likely temporary enslavement—it can be the source of original discoveries in all fields. IV. Every religion must be considered a superstition. A bit of irony. With the same light irony, every superstition must be considered a religion. V. A lot of money is a means of domination. So is too much physical strength (too many adherents to an idea). A name repeated too often as well. All these powers of domination (whether irrational or not) must be rendered impossible. VI. In these deliberations, all of which will be broadcast on television, the government must also be a spectacle, especially comical, rarely tragic, and must always make its decisions in a sort of game that will entertain, by involving the entire populace in a familiar way... Etc."

I. Un homme ne vaut jamais plus qu'un autre homme point ni jamais plus qu'une femme. II. La propriété ne doit pas dépasser certaines bornes. Les objets - jamais les biens - étant interchangeables et ainsi les détenteurs temporels de ces biens, il faut ne jamais déposer sur eux une couche trop épaisse d'affectivité. III. Toute perversion sexuelle n'est pas encouragée: elle est recommandée. Si toute perversion sexuelle s'épanouit comme elle veut - soit dans la liberté soit dans un esclavage consenti et probablement provisoire - elle peut être la source de découvertes originales dans tous les domaines. IV. Toute religion doit être considérée comme une superstition. Un peu d'ironie. Avec la même légère ironie toute superstition doit être considérée comme une religion. V. Beaucoup d'argent est un moyen de domination. Trop de force physique aussi (un trop grand nombre d'adhérents à une idée). Un nom trop souvent répercuté aussi. Tous ces pouvoirs de domination (irrationnels ou non) seront rendus impossibles. VI. Dans ces délibérations qui, toutes seront retransmises par télévision, le gouvernement doit aussi être un spectacle, surtout cocasse, rarement tragique, et toujours prendre ses décisions dans une sorte de jeu qui amusera, en le faisant participer familièrement, tout le peuple… Etc.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Joseph the Provider on the Ernest F. Hodgdon Fund, 1990.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Script of 'Mademoiselle' (1966), a movie adaptation by British director Tony Richardson, with actress Jeanne Moreau in the title role. It was based on a screenplay by Jean Genet, who had written a first version in the form of a short story with dialogues in 1951, with the title 'Les Rêves interdits / l'autre versant du rêve' ('Forbidden Dreams / The Other Side of the Dream').

did.unittitle: Typescript copy of film script in English

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Joseph the Provider on the Ernest F. Hodgdon Fund, 1990.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript mimeograph copy of the post-production film script in English of Mademoiselle (Woodfall Film Presentation Ltd., 1966), dated May 1966.

did.unittitle: Jean Genet papers relating to George Jackson, Angela Davis, and other Black American activists

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Librairie Benoît Forgeot on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2017.

bioghist.head: George Jackson (1941-1971)
bioghist.p: George Jackson (1941-1971) was a Black American author and prison activist. He was killed by a guard at San Quentin Prison in California.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of papers relating to Jean Genet's essay and appeal in support of George Jackson, and other material relating to Genet's support of Black American activists.

Material relating to the production of Genet's statements, "Pour George Jackson" and "Appel pour un comité de soutien aux militants politiques noirs emprisonnés", written in support of George Jackson, John Clutchette, and Fleeta Drumgo, includes: manuscript and typescript drafts with corrections; layouts; printed proofs with autograph corrections; and printed copies.
Also included are lists of persons contacted as part of the appeal, along with circa 300 signed copies of the appeal and letters of support returned to Paule Thévenin, and letters to Paule Thévenin.

Additional items in the collection include: material relating to Genet's preface for L'Assassinat de George Jackson (Paris : Gallimard, 1971); a typescript essay, with autograph corrections, by Thévenin, on George Jackson and his letters from prison; a typescript essay, by an unidentified author, on the deaths of George Jackson and his brother Jonathan; a photocopy of Jean Thibaudeau's essay, "George Jackson et la Revolution"; a letter to Genet from Comité d'action et d'information pour la défense des droits des Noirs Américains, suggesting the coordination of their actions relating to Angela Davis and the Black Panthers; copies of a printed appeal for Angela Davis, by the Comité national pour la défense et la liberation de Angela Davis; a typescript letter, signed, by Morris N. Placere, along with a photocopy of an article by him from November 20, 1974; and a reproduction of Jacques Derrida's letter to Genet, "Lettre à Jean Genet (Fragments) le 20 Août 1971".

bioghist.head: Christopher Stamp (1942-2012)
bioghist.p: Christopher Thomas Stamp (London, 1942–New York, 2012). British music producer and manager known for co-managing and producing such musical acts as the Who and Jimi Hendrix in the 1960s and 1970s and co-founding Track Records. He later became a psychodrama therapist based in the state of New York.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: In 1975, Christopher Stamp—one of the original managers of the rock band The Who, and brother of actor Terence Stamp—commissioned French author Jean Genet to write a screenplay of his autobiographical novel Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs , 1943. The film was to feature the British musician, David Bowie.

Genet spent a month in London writing the new script, which he titled Divine , and while it incorporated several characters from Notre-Dame-des-Fleurs , most notably Divine (the role Bowie was to assume), he re-situated the story to occupied Vichy France during the final months of World War II, and included both German and American soldiers in the narrative. The film was never made because of funding issues. The screenplay disappeared from view.

The collection acquired by the Beinecke Library in 2023, came directly from the Stamp family. It consists of two bound notebooks (from the English brand Maxwell's) and a separate sheaf of loose manuscript pages, written in French, in Genet's hand, and annotated throughout in multiple inks. The inscriptions on the inside covers of the notebooks stipulate explicitly that Genet was the author of the texts, what stage in the process the drafts occurred, and who owned the material.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Christopher Stamp. By descent to his daughter, Amie Stoppard. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Amie Stoppard, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Collection of autograph drafts of the screenplay for Divine . London, 1975.
Material contains approximately 425 pages total; written in red and black ink, with extensive emendations and additions; page fore edges decoratively speckled with blue. Includes:
- Two bound notebooks, both approximately half-filled; many pages loose, with numerous additional leaves inserted between. Volumes bound in brown cloth boards with plain canvas spines.
- A set of 38 loose leaves held separately.
- Also, a bank note from Brussels, signed by Genet, dated October 17, 1975, transferring money to Jacques Maglia.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In the smaller of the two notebooks Genet wrote, "Première ébauche du scénario 'Divine'. Appartient à M. Christopher Stamp je l'ai écrit [...] le mois d'octobre 1975. M. Stamp peut le vendre. Fait à Londres le 5 novembre 1975. Jean Genet". In a postscript below his signature, Genet added, "Mais pendant un an, c'est à dire jusqu'au 5 nov. 76 je dois pouvoir le consulter, soit chez M. Stamp, soit ailleurs. J. G."

("First draft of the script 'Divine,' belonging to Mr. Christopher Stamp. I wrote it by hand in October 1975. Mr. Stamp can sell it. Drawn up in London, November 5, 1975. Jean Genet" "But for one year, until November 5, '76, I can refer back to it either at Mr. Stamp's, or elsewhere. J. G.")

This notebook contains 80 pages of original autograph text, all enumerated at the top right corner of each leaf, and mostly on the rectos, with writing also present on versos, often as replacement text for either the facing or preceding page. There are extensive annotations and emendations throughout. This initial draft's narrative is primarily chronological in nature, with no additional leaves interspersed between the bound pages.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The inscription in the second, larger notebook includes the same postscript, but Genet changed the preceding text to reflect the more evolved state of the text within: "This script, initially titled 'Divine' was written entirely in my hand. It goes from page 1 to page F, with also the pages inserted within. I have given it to Mr. Christopher Stamp, and thus belongs to him. In some form, this script is destined to be made into a film of which he is to be the producer, if that's how the wind blows. He can also sell it to a third party. It's his. Drawn up in London November 4, 1975."

This notebook comprises the longest known autograph draft of Divine , over 140 pages total, and includes significant plot and narrative changes throughout; it is a work in progress and there is a combination of dialogue, outlines, and notes.
Note: for ease of use and preservation purposes, loose pages within this notebook have been housed in separate folders and placed directly after the notebook.

The narrative in this notebook is more complex and comprises what appear to be four distinct draft sections, including a short epilogue. The first, mainly bound-in set, is the most complete version, totaling 66 numbered pages, with an additional 12 labeled A–J. But as Genet notes in the inscription, there are also many loose leaves inserted between the bound pages, approximately 65 in all—mainly torn from the notebooks, with some on graph paper. While several are just single sheets, often revisions of the one before, there are two larger groupings that appear to constitute either earlier drafts of the beginning of the script, or alternate versions, one totaling nearly 30 pages, and the latter 25. Most of the pages are chronologically enumerated.
The overall length of the draft suggests it may be the last version written by hand before it was transcribed to typescript.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A set of 38 original autograph pages held separately from the two notebooks.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A bank note from Brussels, signed by Genet, transferring £1,700 on October 17, 1975, to Jacques Maglia, a former race car driver who was his lover at the time, and one of his heirs and the executor of his estate after his death.

As the bank note is included with the material and dated just two weeks prior to Genet's inscriptions in the notebooks, it could be that this sum was in part, if not in whole, the sum paid by Stamp to Genet for the screenplay.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Essay on Dostoevsky's novel in which Genet analyzes the structure and the journey of the characters, from Alyosha to the illegitimate brother, Smerdyakov.
Published posthumously in La Nouvelle Revue Française , no. 405, October 1986, before being included in L'Ennemi déclaré ( Oeuvres complètes, tome VI , Gallimard, 1991).

custodhist.head: Custodial History

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 6 sheets, in black ink, with a few deletions and corrections.

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Le Langage de la muraille: cent ans jour après jour is the television script for a series featuring the Mettray penal colony in France, where Genet was committed at age 15. The series was commissioned by national television channel Antenne 2 in 1981, with Bertrand Tavernier as director. Genet called off the project before completion.

The historical drama spans 100 years of La Colonie Agricole et Pénitentiaire de Mettray and features prominently its founder and director, Frédéric-Auguste Demetz. Scenes depict social control at the penal institution, national and international historical events, and colonial exploitation and rule in French territories.

bioghist.head: La Colonie Agricole et Pénitentiaire de Mettray
bioghist.p: La Colonie Agricole et Pénitentiaire de Mettray was a juvenile detention center founded jointly in 1839 by magistrate Frédéric-Auguste Demetz (1796–1873) and local landowner Hermann Brétignières de Courteilles (1797–1852). The penal institution was controlled by La Société Paternelle, a private company set up by Demetz. While the institution's official mission sought to rehabilitate young delinquents through vocational training, the reformatory restricted access to learning, used physical punishment as a means of control, and exploited the labor of inmates. The reformatory was endorsed by the French state, which sent youth to Mettray and recruited inmates to serve in colonial wars. It closed in 1939.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of research material, manuscripts and typescripts of "Langage de la muraille", and correspondence with Danièle Delorme/Société Témoins, the film producer.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Preparatory documents and early autograph draft bound in one volume, in black half-morocco leather with patterned paper-covered boards with matching slipcase.

The autograph manuscript is an early version of the script. The first two-thirds of the manuscript contain scenes that do not appear in the accompanying script. Scenes between 1905 and 1936, correspond to the end of the script. The last eight pages recount events from the closure of Mettray until 1940 at the arrival of German troops and were not retained in the typescript volumes.

Includes a two-page preamble: "Pourquoi Mettray?", with the autograph mention "Retourner à Jean Genet"; preparatory notes: the list of work consulted including books about Mettray (one and half page), and a timeline of events from 1815 to the reign of Louis-Philippe Ier, King of the French from 1830 to 1848, with crossed out text or pages containing only a date or brief autograph note.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript that includes autograph additions and corrections, bound in two volumes in black half-morocco leather with patterned paper-covered boards with matching slipcases. The volumes contain photocopies of the typescript renumbered 5-261, annotated by Genet.

The typescript is a work in progress and an early draft of the script, as suggest notes within the script: "encore quelques renseignements et les descriptions des scènes viendront, c'est sûr" ("gathering information and scene descriptions will come later") (p. 29), "Me renseigner mieux" ("will get additional information") (p. 200).
Volume 1 includes an undated original pencil drawing of the author (19 x 27.7 cm).
First page contains autograph title "Le lange de la muraille. Scénario définitif. Cent ans jour pour jour." Pages numbered 5-85.
Title page "Le Langage de la muraille. 2ème cahier. Le retour en arrière, ou le pourquoi de la situation" precedes page 66.
Volume 2 contains autograph title "Seconde Partie, de 1848-1940", pages numbered 86-261.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: History of the work
Esquisse en 28 stations was an idea for an opera libretto, intended as a collaboration with Pierre Boulez (1925–2016), French composer and conductor.
Boulez had been interested in composing an opera since the mid-1960s, and entertained the idea of collaborations with Claudel, Brecht, Beckett, and Genet.

It appears that Genet and Boulez discussed the project around 1967 and continued in the 1970s. In 1984, Patrice Chéreau, a friend of Boulez who had directed Genet's Les Paravents the year prior, renewed Genet's interest in writing a libretto. Six letters from Genet to Pierre Boulez, dated 1984, are held in the Fonds Boulez at the Bibliothèque nationale de France.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photocopy of manuscript comprised of 44 sheets from a 50-sheet Velin Renaissance notepad. It includes:
- Front cover with autograph titles: "Esquisse de 28 stations. [Title crossed out: Fête. Les Funérailles de Churchill]. Les fiançailles de Chch"
- Autograph note from Genet who calculates the duration of the piece: "environ 3 heures d'opéra"

- 41 autograph pages (photocopies) describing the scenes of 24 stations. Include the characters: "Armand (nom provisoire), le coiffeur, le client, le 1er maréchal, le 2ème maréchal, jeune homme, juge, procureur, Georges, …"

... Sheet music of "Cantique de Noël" (O Holy Night). Undated. Original copy of "Nouvelle edition, Cantique de Noël, musique de Adolphe Adam, paroles de Cappeau de Roquemaure. Henri Lemoine & Cie Editeurs". With autograph note on verso: "Samedi 12 février".
Note: This document was found alongside other material and its relationship to a specific work is not established.

It consists of personal and professional papers including letters, autograph notes on various supports, envelopes fragments, manuscripts, financial documents, publishing contracts, newspaper clippings, etc.

did.unittitle: Travel permit issued to Marc Barbezat during Vichy regime

bioghist.head: France during World War II
bioghist.p: Following the terms of the Armistice of June 22, 1940, France was divided into two zones: the German-occupied zone in the north and along the Atlantic coast, including Paris; and the so-called "Free Zone" (Zone libre) in the south, under the authority of the Vichy government, aligned with Nazi Germany and led by Marshal Philippe Pétain. Albi was in the Free Zone, but due to the Vichy regime's restrictions on movement, traveling to Albi required official authorization.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Laisser Passer", official authorization to travel to and return from Albi, issued in the name of Marc Barbezat, August 7, 1940.

did.unittitle: Envelopes addressed to Marc Barbezat and autograph notes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 5 envelopes addressed to Marc Barbezat and 2 autograph notes with addresses.

did.unittitle: Correspondence related to Marc Barbezat and Jean Genet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 autograph letters from Jean-François Pontalis (May 5, 1944; undated).
1 telegram from Olga Barbezat to her husband (April 4, 1945).
1 autograph letter from an unidentified correspondent (April 20, 1945).
1 typed letter, signed, from Gaston Gallimard, with envelope (Paris, October 12, 1945). Kindly inquiring about size of past print runs and retail price of publications by Genet.

1 photocopy of a letter from Genet to Barbezat concerning a possible reissue of Le Miracle de la Rose by the Sagittaire editions of Léon-Pierre Quint and a handwritten note "15% sur 20,000 ex…" (circa 1946).
Draft of a letter from Barbezat to Mouloudji about a play the actor submitted to him titled Isabelle, which he critiques, stating that he cannot publish it as it is (February 3, 1947; 2 pages).

1 typed letter, signed, from Jean-Jacques Pauvert regarding the publication of Les Bonnes (Sceaux, June 30, 1947; 1 page). Also the program for the Athénée-Théatre Louis Jouvet presenting Les Bonnes by Jean Genet and L'Apollon de Marsac by Giraudoux, with a ticket for the critics' preview on April 22, 1947.
1 typed letter, signed, from CIMURA, an agency in Paris representing Genet for foreign performances (July 9, 1947). Requesting 6 copies of Les Bonnes.
1 autograph letter, signed, from Francois Renoux, asking to be put in contact with Jean Genet regarding Les Bonnes (August 5,1947).

2 typed letters, one addressed to Jean Genet, the other to Marc Barbezat, signed, from B.J. Buber, a literary agent in Paris (January 8, 1948). Regarding securing an option for an Italian translation of Notre-Dame des Fleurs and Le Miracle de la Rose (1948).
Instructions for sending a money order to Robert Bernard in Morocco to his address rue Pasquier and a postcard from Robert Bernard to Jean Genet, in an envelope, dated June 1, 1956, from Algiers.

did.unittitle: Administrative and financial documents related to Jean Genet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Handwritten note to "Mr. Barbezat" indicating that "Mr. Jean Genest" (sic) requests that Barbezat calls him back and sends him a money order to the Hotel du Louvre, Paris.
Notarized receipt for 7,500 francs held in escrow related to a publishing contract with Genet (July 2, 1946; 1 page).
Typewritten note in the name of Marc Barbezat acting as guarantor for the repayment of a loan for the purchase of an apartment in Paris by Marius Sénemaud, March 1957.
Administrative documents concerning Jean Genet's income taxes for 1959 and an amount owed to Genet by Barbezat, including a letter from Barbezat to the tax collector and a decision of release.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Newspaper clippings: "Quatre nouveaux poèmes de Jean Genet…", July 5, 1945; "A tout hazard" June 4, 1946; "Sartre et Cocteau demandent la grâce de Genet" July 23, 1948; "…Héliogabale de Jean Genet…" August 7, 1948.

did.unittitle: Reproduction of a lithograph by Émile Picq

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Reproduction of the lithograph by Émile Picq, an illustration for the cover of Chants secrets (1945).

did.unittitle: Jean Genet, autograph notes and fragments

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Marc Barbezat. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2024.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
The group includes a poem by Jean Genet and notes about Pompes funèbres,
Le Balcon , Les Nègres , and Les Paravents .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Ange Soleil, un Martiniquais…" [individual who murdered his wife, Séverine Joram, in 1933]

did.unittitle: Jean-François [Pontalis] to Genet, autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fourques, May 1944, discussing Genet's recent release, his own departure to Jean Hugo's in Fourques in the Hérault, and his admiration for Le Miracle de la Rose [a novel still unpublished at that time] (2 pages).

did.unittitle: Letter fragment, Marc Barbezat to Jean Genet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 pages, in pencil. Written after reading Miracle de la Rose : « Tes héros sont vraiment les enfants des anges, d'une race inconnue » ("Your heroes are truly the children of angels, from an unknown race").

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph notes by Jean Genet (on various materials, envelope fragments, calendar page...), addressed to Barbezat, including addresses in Lake Como or Pergine (Italy), contact information for literary agent Rosica Colin in New York, the English title for Notre-Dame des Fleurs ("Gutter in the sky") with the name of an American film production company.

did.unittitle: Autograph envelope fragments addressed to Marc Barbezat

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 8 envelope fragments addressed to Marc Barbezat in Décines (Isère) from Jean Genet, sent between 1948 and 1956, from Paris, Munich, Innsbruck, Bratislava, etc.

did.unittitle: Autograph note, list of detention houses

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: List of 54 detention houses and 4 educational institutions in France (as of March 1944). 1 page, on the verso of a typewritten note from the Gignoux frères & Barbezat pharmaceutical company.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: List of books, including some of Genet's own works, probably requested by Barbezat: Lorca ( Amours de Don Perlimplín ), Kafka ( Parables ), Rimbaud's poems, Chants secrets , Notre-Dame des Fleurs (2 copies), Miracle de la Rose (2 copies), all issues of the magazine L'Arbalète, etc. On the verso of a typescript fragment from Les Nègres).

did.unittitle: Autograph poem manuscript by Jean Genet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In free verse. 1 page:

« Le bagne a changé de place Son nom a disparu Mais on l'a remplacé par une prison immense. Son nom est Fontevrault. Ce qui veut dire tombeau… […] Dans une triste prison aux murailles noircies Deux jeunes prisonniers, lentement, tournent en rond Ils ont la tête basse sous l'habit d'infamie Numéro sur le bras comme de vrais forçats... »

"The penal colony has moved Its name has disappeared And it has been replaced by an immense prison. Its name is Fontevrault. Which means tomb... […] In a sad prison with blackened walls Two young prisoners, slowly, walk in circles Their heads down beneath the garb of infamy Number on their arm like real convicts"

did.unittitle: Pompes funèbres ( Funeral Rites ), autograph note

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Half page autograph note about Pompes funèbres , also titled L'Œil de Gabès [for which a typewritten draft had been given to Barbezat, before Genet published his novel clandestinely, with Gallimard, in 1947]:

« La construction même du roman de Jean Genet L'Œil de Gabès rend impossible la publication d'un passage complet sans coupure. Pourtant, une certaine unité du ton, sinon de l'intrigue peut être obtenue par la présentation des fragments qui suivent. L'intrigue n'a plus aucun sens, le ton seul importe. Donc pas d'autre titre possible que le titre général de l'ouvrage L'Œil de Gabès. » ("The very structure of Jean Genet's novel L'Œil de Gabès makes it impossible to publish a complete passage without cuts. However, a certain unity of tone, if not of plot, can be achieved by presenting the following fragments. The plot no longer matters; only the tone does. Therefore, there is no other possible title than the general title of the work L'Œil de Gabès.")

did.unittitle: "Comment jouer Le Balcon ", typescript, and autograph note

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 typewritten pages of the text published as the preface under the title "Comment jouer Le Balcon " ("How to Perform The Balcony ") in the 1968 edition ( Œuvres complètes, vol. IV) .
Autograph note. List of characters (some crossed out), for Le Balcon. 1 page, in green ink, on the reverse of a carbon copy fragment from the play).

did.unittitle: Les Nègres ( The Blacks ), autograph note

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Instructions for the layout of the first pages of the original edition of Les Nègres (1958), including the dedication to Abdallah, with Barbezat's note "agreed" in the margin (on the reverse of a letter fragment from Barbezat to Genet, after seeing Roger Blin's production: « Votre pièce a gagné encore à la scène. Nous sommes emballés » ("Your play gained even more on stage. We are thrilled"). Also, on the front and back of a torn card, a trial of the title and Genet's name, in charcoal, intended for the book's cover.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 3 pages, blue ink on lined paper. Genet announces the death of the Italian-style theater and he imagines future theaters, with ten thousand seats, resembling ancient Greek theaters, where the audience can move around. « En écrivant cette pièce, je la voyais représentée dans ce théâtre de plein air. Les gradins taillés dans la pente d'une colline, ne seraient que des bancs de terre, la scène au fond et les décors (les paravents) se détachant sur les arbres d'une futaie ». ("As I wrote this play, I saw it performed in this open-air theater. The stands, carved into the slope of a hill, would be nothing more than earth benches, with the stage at the back and the scenery (the screens) standing out against the trees of the forest.") He then sets out his wishes for declamation, sounds, drawings that the actors must create themselves, costumes, utensils, and objects...
Also a Letter from Marc Barbezat to Genet, with Genet's autograph annotations in blue ink.
December 19, [1961] (2 pages, L'Arbalète letterhead). On the eve of the printing of Les Paravents, Barbezat asks Genet to confirm and clarify certain points in a few lines of dialogue.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inscribed on front cover "Les Nègres / Maroc / Clientèle /Bravo! / …"

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

did.unittitle: Autograph list for a press service

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inscribed "Avec hommages de l'auteur". The list includes the names of François Truffaut, Claude and Natalie Sarraute, Leonor Fini, Kot Jelenski, Ionesco, Beckett, Gaston Gallimard, André Masson, Jean Cau, Aimé Césaire, Giacometti… With the instruction to send a copy of the Japanese edition to His Excellency Nkrumah, President of the Republic of Ghana [from 1960 to 1966].

did.unittitle: Autograph note on a copy of L'Atelier d'Alberto Giacometti

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The group includes:

- Autograph notes from Genet to Barbezat on a mock-up for the second edition of L'Atelier d'Alberto Giacometti (1963). Copy without cover and unbound, with an annotation on the endpaper page: "D'accord. Et aussi pour la pagination des Nègres. Mais ça va faire beaucoup de publications et Gaston Gallimard va être furieux car il n'a pas encore mon Rembrandt. Amitiés. Jean Genet.» ("Agreed. And also for the pagination of Les Nègres. But that's going to result in many publications, and Gaston Gallimard will be furious because he still doesn't have my Rembrandt. Best wishes. Jean Genet.") [The Secret of Rembrandt was published in 1968]. There is a cut on the title page and an autograph addition: "Photographies by Ernest Scheidegger".
- A postcard (view of Granada) with autograph annotation: name and address of Giacometti at 14 [sic] rue Hippolyte Maindron in the 14th arrondissement.
- A fragment of an envelope addressed to Marc Barbezat, posted from Greece.

did.unittitle: Jean Genet to Marc Barbezat, autograph note

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: - Signed autograph note from Jean Genet to Barbezat, on a typed letter from L'Arbalète publishing house, Paris, February 21, 1986, with an envelope. Genet acknowledges receipt of checks for the reissue of Notre-Dame des Fleurs .

- Fragment of the cover of the journal L'Arbalète inscribed: « tâchez de trouver un bon fil à plomb. Je hais le désordre ! » ("try to find a good plumb line. I hate disorder!") referring to uncentered text.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence and materials related to the development of the exhibition "La rupture", conceived and organized by Antoine de Bary for the association Visages, with contributions by Jean Genet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence between Antoine de Bary and Jean Genet, 1982-1983, related to the exhibition "La Rupture" that took place at the Maison des arts et loisirs du Creusot, in March 1983.

1982 June 27. Autograph letter from Antoine de Bary presenting his exhibition project about immigration and colonialism. He asks Genet to write the text for the catalogue. Includes 28 photocopies of working permits and ID cards delivered by the French colonial government in Morocco to "indigenous workers" in 1939.
1982 August 26, Autograph letter from Antoine de Bary to Jean Genet. Bary reports on the development of his exhibition project and kindly asks Genet's input.
1982 September 8. Autograph letter from Antoine de Bary to Jean Genet who joins a typed version of Genet's text for final review.
[1982 August-September]. Autograph drafts and typed text with corrections, by Jean genet.
1983 February 7, 14. Autograph letters and flyer for the exhibition "La Rupture" organized by Association Visages that opened March 19, 1983.

did.unittitle: Jean Genet to Juan Goytisolo, autograph letter, signed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Regarding the sale of manuscript. Juan Goytisolo is traveling to the United States and Genet entrusts him with the first chapter of a book he is writing titled "Description du réel". Genet asks Goytisolo to sell the manuscript and promises to sell the following chapters to the same buyer. He notes the irony of selling his work in the United States while being banned from entering the country.

did.unittitle: Morihien, Paul and Jean Genet papers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Papers related to publishing circa 1947-1948. See also Series I, L'enfant criminel for additional material related to publishing.

did.unittitle: Contract and correspondence related to Querelle de Brest and other works

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; year of acquisition unknown; sold at auction: Pierre Bergé & associés, Collection Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn sale, Paris, 7 November 2019, lot 245. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2019.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Papers documenting the collaboration between author Jean Genet and publisher Paul Morihien in 1947. Contains:
1 signed contract between Genet and Morihien for the works Querelle de Brest, Heliogabale, Pompes funèbres, and Journal d'un voleur dated 1947 July 26;
5 original ink drawings and 5 photoduplicates of drawings by Jean Cocteau intended as possible cover illustrations for Pompes funèbres;
3 autograph letters, signed, from Jean Genet to Paul Morihien, pressing Morihien for financial compensation.

"Mon Petit Paul, je suis très fâché. Tu m'avais promis le fric dès mon arrivée. J'en ai donc disposé et tu me laisses dans le pétrin. Je trouve ça un peu dégueulasse, car moi je corrige Pompes Funèbres . Et je travaille. Je compte sur toi avant samedi le mandat de 50.000 fr. Encore une fois tu n'es pas régulier. Salut."

("My dear Paul, I am very upset. You had promised me the cash upon my arrival. I planned accordingly, and now you leave me in a mess. I find this pretty low on your part because I am correcting Pompes Funèbres and working hard. I am counting on you to send a 50,000 franc money order before Saturday. Once again, you're not reliable. So long.")

did.unittitle: Contract and correspondence related to 'l'Allemagne au jour le jour'

bioghist.head: History of the work
bioghist.p: Genet traveled to Germany around 1948-1949. He planned to write a book titled L'Allemagne au jour le jour , modeled on Simone de Beauvoir's L'Amérique au jour le jour , published in 1948. However, the project remained unfinished, despite the contract signed with publisher Paul Morihien.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two autograph letters signed "Jean" to Paul Morihien [fall 1948?]:
First letter, 1 page. "Mon cher Paul. Tu dois t'inquiéter? Non, je ne suis toujours pas en prison. Je me passionne pour l'Allemagne, pays de plus en plus attachant…."

Second letter, 1 ¼ page. « Mon cher Paul, Il y a près de 3 semaines que je suis dans ce pays où on se relève du cauchemard [sic]. Mais comme un cauchemard vu avec des yeux éveillés fait sourire je crois que c'est un livre comique que j'aurais écrit. (…) Pas une ville n'est intacte. On les reconstruit toutes mais comme les ouvriers d'ici ne savent ni siffler ni chanter autre chose que des marches, on construit dans la résignation comme si l'on détruisait. Les gens qui me connaissent m'accueillent merveilleusement. Frôler un Français les réconforte et les allège, mais me pèse. Mes amours ? On a ce qu'on veut. Je me demande si tenté par Adenauer, celui-ci… Mais passons. (…) Veux-tu remettre à Jacques Guérin 60.000 frs dans la première quinzaine d'octobre, car il me les a avancés. C'est très important. Car si tu lui payes cela je pourrais lui en demander d'autre afin de rester ici. »

("My dear Paul, It has been almost 3 weeks since I've been in this country where all emerge from a nightmare. But like a nightmare seen with open eyes, I think of it as a comic story that I could have written. (…) Not one city is intact. They are all being rebuilt, but since the workers here can neither whistle nor sing anything but marches, they build in resignation, as if destroying. The people who know me welcome me cheerfully. To mingle with a French person reassures and lightens them, but it weighs on me. My love life? I get what I want. I wonder if approaching Adenauer, would… But let's move on. (…) Would you please give Jacques Guérin 60,000 francs in the first half of October, as he advanced them to me? It's very important. Because if you pay him, I could ask him for more, and I could stay here.")

One publishing contract between Editions Morihien and Jean Genet for a work titled L'Allemagne au jour le jour . Undated, signed only by Jean Genet, with the autograph mention "Lu et approuvé" ("Read and approved"), some lines typed, on a 4-page printed document. Genet's residence is listed as Paris, 12 rue joseph de Maistre.

The contract is in draft form, as it is not fully completed. Several clauses remain blank; only the title of the future book and, in Article III, the percentage given to Genet on the first five thousand copies (10%), which rises to 12% for subsequent copies, are specified.

did.unittitle: Palestinian liberation / "Quatre heures à Sabra et Chatila"

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence and notes following Genet's essay "Quatre heures à Sabra et Chatila", published in January 1983, in which he recounts what he witnessed on September 19, 1982, after entering the Sabra and Shatilah camps, Lebanon, where Palestinian refugees had been massacred by Lebanese Christian militias supported by the Israel Army.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On verso of a typed letter from the Société des Auteurs et Compositeurs Dramatiques dated May 4, 1982. « Une fois de plus les Palestiniens sont un peuple suspendu au-dessus du sol. Sont-ils à nouveau une abstraction qui doit, quel qu'en soit le prix, se concrétiser ? »

bioghist.head: Charles Silvestre (1937– )
bioghist.p: Charles Silvestre (1937– ). French journalist, former editorial director of l'Humanité .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Editor at the French daily newspaper l'Humanité informing Genet that the foreign desk is trying to reach him: "La rubrique de politique étrangère cherche à vous joinder d'urgence au sujet de vos "Quatre heures à Chatila". Pouvez vous m'appeler le plus tôt possible à l'Humanité: 246 82 69 (en mon absence Dominique Bari). A bientôt. Amicalement. Charles Silvestre"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inscribed: "Jean, je viens de lire ton texte sur Chatila. Je t'y retrouve, j'y retrouve ta langue et ton ton. Bien heureux j'avais lu [?] pages. Veux-tu me faire signe lorsque tu passes au Maroc? Amitiés. [signature in Arabic script]"

bioghist.head: Abdelkebir Khatibi (1938-2009)
bioghist.p: Abdelkebir Khatibi (Morocco, 1938-2009). Literary critic, novelist, philosopher, playwright, poet, and sociologist.

did.unittitle: Comité pour la reconnaissance de l'OLP (CNP), typed letter

bioghist.head: Geneviève Clancy (1937–2005)
bioghist.p: Geneviève Clancy (1937–2005). French poet, philosopher, and activist.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typed letter from the CNP to Jean Genet. Also, appeals and documents signed by Geneviève Clancy and Philippe Tancelin, who lead the CNP, in support of the official recognition of the Organisation de libération de la Palestine-OLP (Palestine Liberation Organization). "La France doit reconnaitre l'OLP – Depuis 35 ans, le people palestinien – quatre millions et demi d'hommes et femmes—vit sans patrie, pour moitié dans l'exil, pour moitié sous l'occupation."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains photographs from the 1950s and utilities bills documenting Genet's residence rue des Acacias in Paris, 1981-1982.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 1 black-and-white photographic print; 18 x 12.3 cm. Portrait of Jean Genet by Roger Parry for Gallimard, March 1951. Taken by the banks of the River Seine to promote the release of Genet's Œuvres complètes volume II. Signed by Roger Parry on lower right corner.

1 black-and-white photographic print; 14 x 9 cm. Depicts Genet standing with a friend in a street on the day of the premiere of The Maids ( Les Bonnes ), June 5, 1956, at the New Lindsey Theatre Club in London.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Formerly owned by Geneviève & Jean-Paul Kahn; dates unknown. Purchased by the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library from Librairie Benoît Forgeot, 2023.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Personal documents related to Paris residence:
[1983 January]. Autograph notecard. Building doorman/caretaker sharing best wishes for 1983.
1982 December 16. Bill for phone service (PTT Télécommunications) in the amount of 893,20 francs, addressed to "Mr Brassens D., 47 rue des Acacias, 75017 Paris".
1982 March 26. Notice of shut-off from electric company.
1982 May 16. Receipt for payment of electric bill in the amount of 768,31 francs, cash.
1981 December 1. Receipt for Habitat (furniture store) in the amount of 1403,40 francs.
